title: Diabetes Risk Calculator – Type 2 Diabetes Assessment
tagline: Assess your risk of developing Type 2 diabetes based on age, BMI, family history, and lifestyle factors with a validated point-based scoring algorithm.
intro: Enter your health information below to calculate a personalized Type 2 diabetes risk score and receive evidence-based prevention guidance.

error.selectActivity: Please select a physical activity level.

result.heading: Diabetes Risk Assessment
result.note: This is a screening tool only and does not replace medical diagnosis. Consult your healthcare provider for personalised advice.

examples.heading: Diabetes risk calculator examples
examples.intro: Four sample profiles illustrating different risk levels from low to very high.

items.input: Age 25, BMI 22.5, no family history, regular activity, normal BP, never smoked, no GDM, White
items.note: No modifiable or hereditary risk factors present. Maintain healthy lifestyle.

items.input: Age 50, BMI 27.8, family history, occasional activity, normal BP, former smoker, no GDM, Hispanic
items.note: Age, overweight, family history, and reduced activity elevate risk moderately.

items.input: Age 65, BMI 32.1, family history, sedentary, high BP, current smoker, no GDM, African American
items.note: Multiple compounding factors: obesity, hypertension, family history, inactivity, smoking.

items.input: Age 45, BMI 35.2, family history, sedentary, high BP, current smoker, history of GDM, Asian
items.note: Gestational diabetes history combined with obesity, hypertension, and sedentary lifestyle.

howto.heading: How to use the diabetes risk calculator
steps: Enter your age in years and current BMI (weight in kg divided by height in metres squared).
Select your family history, physical activity level, blood pressure status, and smoking status using the option buttons.
Select whether you have a history of gestational diabetes and your race/ethnicity — both influence baseline risk.
Click 'Calculate Risk' to see your score, risk category, and estimated 10-year risk percentage.
Review the result and the prevention note. Share the output with your healthcare provider at your next check-up.

items.q: What is a Type 2 diabetes risk calculator?
items.a: A Type 2 diabetes risk calculator is a validated screening tool that estimates the likelihood of developing the condition over a defined future period. It weights known clinical and lifestyle risk factors — such as age, BMI, family history, physical inactivity, hypertension, and smoking — into a composite score. The result helps prioritise who should seek further testing such as fasting glucose or HbA1c measurements.

items.q: Why does family history affect diabetes risk?
items.a: Type 2 diabetes has a strong hereditary component. First-degree relatives of someone with the condition have two to three times the population-level risk due to shared genetic variants that impair insulin secretion and sensitivity. Family history also reflects shared lifestyle environments during childhood, compounding the genetic effect.

items.q: How does BMI influence the score?
items.a: Excess adipose tissue — particularly visceral fat — increases circulating free fatty acids and inflammatory cytokines that drive insulin resistance. BMI of 25–29.9 (overweight) roughly doubles diabetes risk compared with the normal range, while BMI ≥ 30 (obese) raises it three- to seven-fold depending on distribution. Even modest weight loss of 5–7% of body weight can substantially reduce risk.

items.q: What is gestational diabetes and why is it a risk factor?
items.a: Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is glucose intolerance first recognised during pregnancy. Women who experience GDM have a 40–60% lifetime probability of developing Type 2 diabetes because GDM reveals underlying pancreatic beta-cell dysfunction and insulin resistance that persists after delivery. Postpartum glucose testing and lifestyle modification are strongly recommended.

items.q: Can lifestyle changes actually reduce my risk?
items.a: Yes — the landmark Diabetes Prevention Program trial showed that structured lifestyle intervention (7% weight loss and 150 minutes of moderate activity per week) reduced progression from prediabetes to Type 2 diabetes by 58%, compared with 31% for metformin. Quitting smoking, improving diet quality, and reducing sedentary time all contribute additional risk reduction independent of weight change.

items.q: Is this calculator a diagnosis tool?
items.a: No. This tool is a risk-stratification aid and cannot diagnose diabetes. A formal diagnosis requires laboratory confirmation — typically a fasting plasma glucose ≥ 126 mg/dL, a 2-hour oral glucose tolerance test result ≥ 200 mg/dL, an HbA1c ≥ 6.5%, or a random glucose ≥ 200 mg/dL with symptoms. Always consult a qualified clinician for diagnostic assessment.

about.heading: About the diabetes risk calculator
about.body:
Type 2 diabetes is one of the most prevalent chronic conditions worldwide, affecting more than 500 million adults and projected to grow to 700 million by 2045. Unlike Type 1 diabetes, which results from autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells, Type 2 is characterised by progressive insulin resistance and relative insulin deficiency — both driven by genetic predisposition, obesity, physical inactivity, and a cluster of other modifiable and non-modifiable risk factors.

The diabetes risk calculator on this page applies a point-based scoring algorithm modelled on validated population-level risk instruments such as the Finnish Diabetes Risk Score (FINDRISC) and the American Diabetes Association (ADA) risk questionnaire. Each risk factor contributes a weighted point value: age adds up to 3 points because insulin sensitivity naturally declines with age and cumulative metabolic insults accumulate over decades; BMI contributes up to 3 points reflecting the dose-response relationship between adiposity and insulin resistance; family history in a first-degree relative adds 2 points to account for the strong hereditary component; physical inactivity adds up to 2 points because skeletal muscle is the primary site of insulin-stimulated glucose uptake and regular activity dramatically improves sensitivity; hypertension adds 2 points as it frequently coexists with the metabolic syndrome cluster; current or former smoking adds up to 2 points since nicotine impairs insulin secretion and promotes central fat deposition; a history of gestational diabetes adds 2 points as it reveals latent pancreatic beta-cell dysfunction; and certain ethnic backgrounds add 1 point due to demonstrated higher population-level incidence even after adjusting for other factors.

Scores of 0–3 place an individual in the Low Risk category, corresponding to an estimated 10-year Type 2 diabetes risk below 5%. Scores of 4–8 indicate Moderate Risk with a 5–15% estimated probability — a range where lifestyle counselling and periodic fasting glucose checks become worthwhile. Scores of 9–14 correspond to High Risk (16–30%), where clinical guidelines recommend referral for structured prevention programmes and baseline HbA1c testing. Scores of 15 or above indicate Very High Risk (greater than 30%), signalling an immediate priority for clinical evaluation and intensive lifestyle or pharmacological intervention.

The tool is designed to be used as a first-line population screening aid, not a clinical diagnostic instrument. A high score does not mean diabetes is present or inevitable — it means the background risk warrants professional evaluation. Conversely, a low score does not guarantee protection, particularly for people with significant centraladiposity or a borderline BMI. The most actionable use of the calculator is as a motivational trigger: seeing a concrete risk number often improves adherence to preventive behaviour change programmes more effectively than general health warnings.

Prevention remains highly effective across all risk categories. The Diabetes Prevention Program, Da Qing Study, Finnish Diabetes Prevention Study, and multiple subsequent trials consistently show that structured lifestyle intervention — targeting at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity physical activity per week and 5–7% reduction in body weight — reduces progression from prediabetes to Type 2 diabetes by 50–60%. Even small improvements in diet quality, reduction of sedentary time, smoking cessation, and better sleep hygiene each contribute independently to lowering risk, making the prognosis genuinely modifiable for the vast majority of people in the moderate-to-high risk range.
